coredata.dc:title: The role of carbon dots in the life cycle of crops
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Baoyan
Liu, Guo
Li, Wei
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
Zhuang, Jianle
Zheng, Mingtao
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: The novel 0-dimensional quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon dots (diameter <10 nm) have the characteristics of small size, adjustable emission wavelength range, easy functional modification of surface groups, and good biocompatibility. Studies showed that it has great application potential in the entire life cycle of crops, including pre-harvest and post-harvest. Subsequently, crop waste can be used to prepare carbon dots and re-enter the crop life cycle. This review summarizes recent studies showing that carbon dots with specific chemical or optical properties can enhance crop yields in different ways. The small size and surface hydrophilic groups that can cross the plant cell barrier are beneficial to CDs to promote seed germination and absorb nutrients to promote growth. The high carbon content (>60%) endows it with potential as a nano-carbon fertilizer. CDs with positive surface charges or prepared by medium molecular weight PEI are the key to targeted delivery of fertilizers, pesticides, fungicides, DNA or RNA. The luminescence range of CDs covers the absorption range of plants and the electron donor and acceptor properties promote plant photosynthesis. The antioxidant properties of CDs are crucial for improving crop stress resistance. Photodynamic, photothermal, and UV absorption properties endow CDs with antibacterial and preservation applications. Finally, current challenges and future prospects are discussed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CDs act as nano-fertilizers to promote plant growth. (a) CDs promote the growth of Rome lettuce as nitrogen fertilizer. (f) CDP introduced functional DNA into plant cells and successfully released and expressed it.

head.ce:title: The role of carbon dots in the life cycle of crops

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel 0-dimensional quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon dots (diameter <10 nm) have the characteristics of small size, adjustable emission wavelength range, easy functional modification of surface groups, and good biocompatibility. Studies showed that it has great application potential in the entire life cycle of crops, including pre-harvest and post-harvest. Subsequently, crop waste can be used to prepare carbon dots and re-enter the crop life cycle. This review summarizes recent studies showing that carbon dots with specific chemical or optical properties can enhance crop yields in different ways. The small size and surface hydrophilic groups that can cross the plant cell barrier are beneficial to CDs to promote seed germination and absorb nutrients to promote growth. The high carbon content (>60%) endows it with potential as a nano-carbon fertilizer. CDs with positive surface charges or prepared by medium molecular weight PEI are the key to targeted delivery of fertilizers, pesticides, fungicides, DNA or RNA. The luminescence range of CDs covers the absorption range of plants and the electron donor and acceptor properties promote plant photosynthesis. The antioxidant properties of CDs are crucial for improving crop stress resistance. Photodynamic, photothermal, and UV absorption properties endow CDs with antibacterial and preservation applications. Finally, current challenges and future prospects are discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the United Nations, the number of hungry people in the world has been slowly increasing since 2014, and projections show that the world will not be able to achieve the goal of zero hunger by 2030 (FAO, 2020). Furthermore, dwindling arable land worldwide, water scarcity, frequent climatic extremes, COVID-19 pandemic in 2019 and current inefficiency in the use of agrochemicals have exacerbated crop stress and reduced crop yields (Zhao et al., 2020a). Additionally, the progress of selecting crop varieties is slow, and the public is highly concerned about the safety of transgenic crops (De Lange et al., 2018). Agriculture needs to be more productive and diversified while coping with climate change and the constraints of limited natural resources. Producing more food with less resources, while protecting and improving farmers' livelihoods, is a global challenge.

Nanotechnology is crucial in meeting this challenge. Nanotechnology involves nanomaterials that are less than 100 nm in at least one dimension and can operate at the atomic or molecular level. The small size of nanomaterials allows them to cross biological barriers, diffuse into plant vascular systems after root or leaf application, and enable new and effective nutrient and pesticide delivery pathways. The structure and surface chemistry of nanomaterials can be modified to provide the desired properties and functions, guide them to the right location in the plant or soil, and provide intelligent release and delivery strategies. Its application in agriculture has been contributive in promoting plant growth and increase crop yields (Giraldo et al., 2019; Gogos et al., 2012; Kah et al., 2019; Lowry et al., 2019; Raliya et al., 2018). Engineering nanoparticles including carbon nanomaterials, metal nanomaterials (Au, Ag, etc.), oxide nanomaterials (TiO2, Al2O3, etc.), dendritic polymer nanomaterials and composite nanomaterials (Khan et al., 2019) have shown good results in seed germination, plant growth and development, pathogen diagnosis, and toxic pesticide detection (Nuruzzaman et al., 2016; Pereira et al., 2021; Shukla et al., 2019). Thus, plant nanobiotics can contribute to sustainable agricultural production through mechanisms different from chemical and genetic engineering (Hu and Xianyu, 2021; Kah et al., 2018, 2019; Lowry et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; White and Gardea-Torresdey, 2018). However, the above nanomaterials have poor water solubility and biocompatibility, which require surface treatment and modification. The complex preparation and high cost greatly limit their popularization and application.

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical zero-dimensional nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. In 2004, when scientists were studying the preparation of single-walled carbon nanotubes by arc discharge, they accidentally discovered carbon nanoparticles with bright fluorescence (Xu et al., 2004). Nano CDs are a new system or carbon material between molecule and condensed solid, which gathers the characteristics of nano effect, surface functional groups and carbon elements. Therefore, it shows three basic functions of optics, chemistry and biology, and has multiple functions and applications (Wang and Lu, 2022; Liu et al., 2020). Compared with inorganic, metallic and oxide-based nanomaterials, CDs have unique advantages, including optical stability, small size, biocompatibility, water solubility, low cost and excellent luminescence performance (Peng et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2015). They have been widely researched in biological fields, such as biological imaging, biosensing and molecular transfer, etc. In recent years, CDs have been applied in agriculture, especially in the field of crops. CDs emited blue or red light, which plants need, so they can be sprayed on leaves or made into supplementary lights to boost crop growth and yield (Guo et al., 2022). In addition, the abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs, small size and other structural characteristics made it show great advantages in promoting crop growth and enhancing photosynthesis and stress resistance (Li et al., 2020b).

Based on their low toxicity and excellent fluorescence properties, CDs were first used for biological imaging of crop systems. The surface amino functionalized CDs were prepared using p-phenylenediamine as a precursor (Li et al., 2016). The red fluorescent CDs were transferred from root to stem and leaf by the vascular system through exoplasmic pathways and tended to be crosslinked and clustered in exoplasmic space. Subsequently, Li et al. (2017) further dispersed the above CDs in a cellulose matrix, thus inhibiting the non-radiative decay process and avoiding the fluorescence quenching of CDs aggregation, and successfully applied them to the fluorescence imaging of onion epidermis tissue, mung bean root, and Arabidopsis stem and leaf. The studies of CDs acting throughout the life cycle of crops have also been reported. Li et al. (2018a) systematically studied the effects of CDs on the entire growth process of rice and found that CDs promoted plant growth by promoting seed germination, root growth, and carbohydrate accumulation. The yield increased by 14.8%. Eventually, CDs degraded into plant hormone analogs (which promoted plant growth) and carbon dioxide (which was converted to carbohydrates through the Calvin cycle). They then obtained similar results by studying the entire life cycle of CDs on the dicotyledons Arabidopsis thaliana and Trifolium repens L (Li, H. et al., 2019).

On the other hand, using crop waste to prepare CDs has advantages in raw material cost and environmental benefits, which is conducive to the sustainable development of agriculture. Therefore, it has attracted extensive attention and research. The design, synthesis, characteristics and application of multifunctional CDs prepared from biomass waste have been reviewed recently (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021).

The role of CDs in the whole crop life cycle mainly includes pre-harvest process (seed germination, nano-fertilizer, plant growth regulator, targeted delivery of fertilizers, pesticides and biomolecules, photosynthesis, and stress tolerance), and post-harvest process (anti-bacterial preservation of harvested fruits, intelligent anti-counterfeit packaging). At the end of the life cycle of the crops they become crop wastes, which are good biomass materials, and can be used to prepare CDs and re-enter the crop life cycle, thus forming a closed loop ( Fig. 1).

At present, the research on the effects of CDs on crops is mainly focused on the pre-harvest process. In this review, the effect of CDs on the whole life cycle of crops was summarized, including pre-harvest process, post-harvest process, and finally returning to the cycle by preparing CDs from crop waste. The possible future research directions of CD in crops were also discussed. This review may shed light on the interaction between CDs and crops, so that researchers can germinate more scientific ideas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs promote seed germination of crops
ce:section.ce:para: Germination is the beginning of plant individual development and the basis of crop quality. The seeds have high vigor, and rapid germination and development, which could ensure the timely transportation of water and nutrients, and form neat and robust seedlings (Acharya et al., 2020). Slow germination leads to long-term exposure of seedlings which makes them susceptible to environmental stress, resulting in reduced root vigor and reduced crop yields (Acharya et al., 2019).

As shown in Fig. 2(a), seed germination markedly absorbs water in Phase I. In Phase II, it uses the absorbed water for metabolism, including enzyme synthesis and the conversion of organic matter into nutrients for seedling development. In Phase III, due to the rapid growth of the embryo and the increase in cell volume, a large amount of water is absorbed again.

Pereira et al. (2021) described in detail the principle of seed germination and the role of nanoparticles (Fig. 2(b)). Nanoparticles mainly promote water absorption, regulate the balance between reactive oxygen species (ROS) and plant growth hormones, and induce the response to stress after the seeds develop into plants.

CDs can promote seed germination because there are more hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc.) on the surface of CDs which can promote the absorption of water by seeds (Kou et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2018a; Li, H et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018b). In addition, there are two ways to transport water across the membrane, namely, the diffusion of lipid bilayer and the diffusion of aquaporins. The latter mainly regulates the rapid flow of water in cells (Wang et al., 2019).

Kou et al. (2021b) reported that the accelerated germination of plant seeds under suitable concentration of CDs might be due to the increased water absorption of seeds during the imbibition stage. Further tests suggested that the hydrophilic groups (−COOH, −OH) on the surface of CDs can promote the expression of aquaporin-related genes.

CDs prepared by Li, Y. et al., (2020) was used to accelerate the germination of rice seeds. The effects of salt, strong light and herbicide stress on rice seedlings was systematically studied. Results showed that the fresh weight and plant height of CD-germinated rice seedlings significantly increased compared with control. Further experiments showed that CDs could enhance the plant's own antioxidant defense system and improve the tolerance of plants to adverse growth environments.

These results suggest that CDs treatment can not only promote seed germination, but also promote plant growth and improve stress resistance of plants. This is due to their small size, ease of entry and exit into plant cell walls, hydrophilic groups on their surfaces that facilitate water and nutrient absorption, and their antioxidant properties. The mechanism is mainly focused on the promotion of seed water absorption accompanied by the absorption of nutrients. However, there are a series of enzymes and plant hormones involved in regulating seed germination. Moreover, the specific molecular mechanism of CDs promoting seed germination remains unclear. Therefore, researchers should pay more attention to the changes of related enzymes and plant hormones on the molecular level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs promote the growth and development of crops
ce:section.ce:para: The published studies on the effect of CDs in the pre-harvest process of crops are summarized in Table 1, including the precursors for preparation of CDs, the preparation methods, size of CDs obtained, action concentration, crop species, and the role of CDs, etc.

In order to maintain the normal life activities of crops, timely and appropriate fertilization is needed, including macro elements (nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium) and trace elements (zinc, copper, iron, magnesium, etc.). Excessive fertilization and very low use efficiencies (<50%) (Urso and Gilbertson, 2018) lead to significant nutrient loss and harmful effects on the surrounding soil and surface water.

Nanomaterials (carbon-based nanomaterials, mineral nanomaterials, metals and metal oxide nanomaterials) have been widely studied for their potential to improve the utilization efficiency of nutrients due to their small size, which can effectively transfer nutrients through root, leaf, and seed coatings (Lowry et al., 2019). However, the above nanomaterials have not been effectively applied in practice due to the complex preparation process, high cost, and metal pollutant release.

Nano-carbon fertilizer was considered to have the effect of saving fertilizer and increasing production. CDs is rich in carbon (over 60%), nitrogen, and other essential nutrients for plants, and are economical, environmentally friendly and efficient compared to other fertilizers. These unique advantages are expected to make CDs a new and efficient nano-carbon fertilizer. A large number of studies have shown that they can promote plant moisture and mineral absorption (Wang et al., 2018b), photosynthesis (Wang, C. et al., 2021), nutrient assimilation (Kou et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2018a) and act as a carbon fertilizer to increase production.

CDs with rapeseed pollen as a precursor was synthesized by Zheng et al. (2017) on a large scale at low cost (0.03 dollars/g) and was used as carbon fertilizer. It can significantly promote the growth of hydroponic Rome lettuce ( Fig. 3(a)). Under the optimal treatment of 30 mg/L, the biomass of Rome lettuce was significantly increased by 48.09% compared with the control group (Zheng et al., 2017). Subsequently, citric acid and thiourea were quickly reacted in a short time (10 min) at a mild temperature (120 °C) to obtain nitrogen-doped CDs (Wang, A. et al., 2019), which were used as a fertilizer for hydroponic mung beans growth. A suitable concentration (0.2 mg/mL) could increase the average stem and root length of mung beans by 200% and 17.46% (Fig. 3(b)), but a high concentration of CDs (4 mg/mL) could prevent the germination of mung beans (Fig. 3(c)).

The above research indicates that CDs with appropriate concentrations can be used as fertilizer. But the above research is a hydroponic plant method and the practical application still needs a large number of field trials to support it. Nitrogen is abundant in the natural environment and is a good source of fertilizer but cannot be utilized by higher plants directly. About 90% of the nitrogen in the atmosphere is biologically fixed by microorganisms. Nitrogen-fixing microorganisms have nitrogen-fixing enzymes in their bodies, which can reduce molecular nitrogen to ammonia.

To meet the demand for nitrogen fertilizer, it is vital to improve the nitrogen fixation activity for nitrogen-fixing bacteria. Wang et al. (2018a) treated Azotobacter chroococcum with 4 μg/mL CDs prepared by electrolytic graphite rod, and their nitrogen-fixing activity increased by 158% (Fig. 3(d)). CDs can combine with the nitrogenase, affect the secondary structure of nitrogenase, improve the electron transfer in the biocatalytic process, and finally improve the nitrogenase activity (Fig. 3(e)).

CDs, as a nanoscale material, whether used as fertilizer itself or as a delivery and transportation carrier of fertilize, may have better absorption efficiency and effectiveness than other carbon components. Further research is needed to determine whether CDs are more effective than other carbon constituents or work in the form of nanoparticles.

CDs have been widely studied as plant growth regulators. For example, Chen et al. (2020a) suggested that CDs with different surface functionalities have important effects on plant growth regulation. They investigated the role of functional carbon nano-points (FCNs) with multiple chemical groups as plant growth regulators on Arabidopsis thaliana media and found that the carboxyl and ester groups on the surface of FCNs and their quantum sizes were the most critical factors for promoting seedling growth because they had perfect water dispersion, nutrient adsorption capacity, and biocompatibility.

In addition to the role of CDs themselves as plant growth regulators, CDs in plants could be degraded into plant hormone analogs by horseradish peroxidase in the presence of hydrogen peroxide to promote plant growth ( Li et al., 2018a; Li, H et al., 2019). In general, plant hormones have the common structural characteristics of aromatic nuclei, ethers, and carboxyl groups (Went and Thimann, 1937).

It is worth noting that plant growth substances are divided into phytohormones, which occur naturally in plants, and plant growth regulators which are synthetic (Wang et al., 2019). Plant growth regulators have similar activities to plant hormones and can be divided into three types, including plant growth promotors, plant growth inhibitors, and plant growth retardants. Commonly used growth promoters are auxin (indolebutyric acid, α-naphthalene acid), gibberellins (gibberellins), cytokinins (6-benzyladenine, 6-furfurylaminopurine), ethylene (2-chloroethyl phosphonic acid), etc (Wang et al., 2019). When studying the effects of CDs as plant growth regulators, attention should be paid to the comparison with the commercial substances mentioned above.

Nanomaterials have been developed to load drugs or active substances for delivery to targeted sites in the living body. A large number of targeted drug deliveries and cancer diagnoses and treatment have been carried out (Gu et al., 2011). Although the same principle can be applied to plant systems, the application of nanomaterials in plant systems has received little attention at present. CDs have been reported to deliver biomolecules in animal cells, but few studies have been carried out in plant systems.

PEI is a cationic polymer with high DNA-loading capacity and is often used as a good vector for rapid transfection. But it is highly toxic. CDs was reported to enhance the transfection efficiency of target genes in plant cells, and their advantages such as non-toxicity, environmental protection and simple operation make them show great potential in agricultural applications. Positively charged CDs were prepared by using PEI as a precursor and combined with negatively charged nucleic acids to form complex CD-PEI/pDNA ( Fig. 4(a)) (Liu et al., 2012), which can mediate the transfection and expression of plasmid DNA in COS-7 and HepG2 cells (Fig. 4(b)). CDs-functionalization with branched PEIs (bPEIs) delivery of plasmid DNA to animal cells is relatively easy (Liu et al., 2012; Pierrat et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2014), whereas plant cells have cell walls and are limited by entry modes and sizes of a range of substances (Fig. 4(e)) (Wang et al., 2016), the size of the CDs appeared to be an important variable in determining its efficacy. Schwartz et al. (2020) studied the effect of CDs prepared by PEI of different molecular weights as small interfering RNA carrier on the silencing effect of GFP fluorescent protein. The transfection method of spraying nanocomposites on tobacco leaves was simpler than the conventional transfection method and saved time and effort. Interestingly, CDs prepared with medium molecular weight PEI were found to have the highest silencing activity (Fig. 4(c)). This suggests that small size may be a prerequisite for efficient transmission through plant cell walls, but is not the only factor affecting silencing efficiency, and subsequent barriers to transmission such as entosis through the plasma membrane, escape from the intimal system, and release of RNA, might also affect silencing efficiency (Fig. 4(d)) (Schwartz et al., 2020). Subsequently, Wang et al. (2020) reported a nanocomposite combining PEI with CDs that could deliver the hydamycin resistance gene into the rice and induce hygromycin resistance in rice. Meanwhile, the β-glucuronidase gene was delivered into mature rice embryo induced callus by the nanocomposite (Fig. 4(f)).

CDs can be used as a carrier to deliver DNA or RNA into plants, but there may still be a certain distance to achieve true targeting. In addition, unlike gene editing, current studies have shown that CDs only act as a carrier for delivering biological molecules without changing the sequence of DNA or RNA.

Photosynthesis is the most important chemical reaction on earth and is a complex process involving photochemical reactions and material transformations. Chloroplasts are organelles for photosynthesis, mainly for light and carbon reactions ( Fig. 5(a)) (Wang et al., 2019). The light reaction takes place on the thylakoid membrane of chloroplast, and the light energy is absorbed by the concentrating pigment and transferred to the reaction center through induced resonance. The special chlorophyll a pairs in the reaction center separates the redox charge caused by light absorption and directly converts the light energy into chemical energy. The absorption of light-generated electrons by pigments through a series of electron and proton transfers in photosystem II (PS II) and photosystem I (PS I), combined with photophosphorylation, leads to the formation of NADPH and ATP (assimilation force). Carbon reactions occur in the chloroplast stroma, where assimilation forces reduce CO2 and promote its assimilation into organic matter. The Calvin cycle (C3 pathway) is the main form of carbon assimilation.

According to theoretical calculation, the efficiency of plant light energy conversion into biomass is only about 1% at present, and there is a large space for improvement of photosynthesis efficiency (Wang et al., 2019). CDs have excellent optical properties, can be used as both electron donors and acceptors, and their surfaces are easy to be functionally modified, which provides favorable conditions for their applications in crops photosynthesis. In 2014, Chandra et al. (2014) first proposed the photoinduced electron transfer process of photosynthetic system/photo-capture complex based on CDs. Subsequently, the promotion mechanism of dual-emission CDs on plant photosynthesis was systematically studied by Li, W. et al. (2018).

Chlorophyll is one of the important photosynthetic pigments in higher plants. It is arranged on the thylakoid membrane and mainly consists of chlorophyll A and chlorophyll B. Chlorophyll contains four pyrrole rings, which are linked to four methylenes to form a large ring called a porphyrin ring. Chlorophyll is a giant conjugated system that is the main photosynthetic pigment. Chlorophyll does not participate in hydrogen transfer, only in the way of electron transfer and resonance transfer, to participate in the light reaction. Chlorophyll is extremely capable of absorbing light, including red light with wavelengths of 640–660 nm and blue light with wavelengths of 430–450 nm (Wang et al., 2019).

CDs have excellent luminescence properties, while the solar radiation that plants can use is in a relatively narrow range of wavelength. Therefore, researchers have prepared functionalized CDs which can absorb ultraviolet (UV) light that plants cannot absorb and convert it into blue-violet light or red light that plants can use to increase the photosynthetic rate of leaves (Sai et al., 2018) and thus promote plant growth. This functional characteristic of CDs was called artificial antenna by researchers (Li et al., 2020a; Wang, C et al., 2021).

Chlorophyll content is an important index of photosynthetic efficiency which has been measured in many studies. However, the results obtained are not consistent. For example, when the study subjects were all dicotyledons, the chlorophyll content increased when CDs were applied to mung beans (Wang et al., 2018b; Zhang et al., 2018) and tomato (Kou et al., 2021), while chlorophyll content did not increase when CDs were applied to mung beans (Li, H. et al., 2019). When CDs prepared by electrolysis was applied to monocotyledons of rice, the chlorophyll content did not increase compared with the control, but the final yield of rice increased by 14.8% (Li et al., 2018a).

The above inconsistent results may be related to the types and concentrations of CDs, and the different light saturation points of different plants. Chloroplasts reabsorb light emitted by CDs with different quantum yields and concentrations for photosynthesis, which increases electron transfer rates in light reactions and generates more energy substances (ATP and NADPH) for CO2 assimilation. However, when the light intensity exceeded the light saturation point, the photosynthetic rate no longer increased, the electron transfer reaction and Rubiso activity became the limiting factors, and the CO2 metabolism cannot be synchronized with the light absorption, resulting in excessive electron transfer to oxygen molecules, thereby generating ROS to destroy photosynthetic system (Gao et al., 2019).

The effect of CDs on PS Ⅰ and PS Ⅱ of chloroplasts is also different. Wang et al. (2018b) found that CDs treatment could improve the activity of PS Ⅰ, but not PS Ⅱ. The addition of CDs increased the electron transfer rate in PS Ⅰ, but not in PS Ⅱ. Because PS Ⅰ contained clusters of iron sulfur, but PS Ⅱ did not. CDs could form non-covalent bonds with iron, which was supported by the photoluminescence and lifetime results of CDs. Subsequently, in the study of lettuce and tomato by Kou et al. (2021b), based on the results of the photosystem study, they also hypothesized that CDs could promote photosynthesis by promoting PS I activity, thus improving yield and nutritional value of plants. However, more studies (Chandra et al., 2014; Li et al., 2021a,b; Wang, C et al., 2021; Xiao et al., 2021) believe that photosynthesis is promoted by promoting electron transport in PS Ⅱ. Li et al. (2021b) prepared CDs with different quantum yields, and the results showed that CDs with medium quantum yields could increase electron transfer rate and photosynthetic efficiency of PS Ⅱ, thus promoting the growth of rice seedlings. Further experiments proved that there was no energy transfer between CDs and chloroplasts after excitation in chloroplasts, but the blue fluorescence generated by CDs was reabsorbed by chloroplasts for photosynthesis, thus improving the photosynthetic activity of chloroplasts. Similar results are reported by Xiao et al. (2021).

It's worth noting that there are three photosynthetic electron transport pathways, noncyclic electron transport (NET), cyclic electron transport (CET), and pseudocyclic electron transport (PET). Under normal physiological conditions, PS Ⅱ and PS I are jointly excited by light and connected in series to promote electron transfer, snatching electrons from water and transferring them to NADP+, producing O2 and NADPH+H+ (Fig. 5(b)). This process is called NET pathway. When PS I is photoexcited but PS Ⅱ is not, the electrons generated by PS I are transferred to Fd and returned to PS I via Cyt b6f complex and PC, forming a ring electron transfer around PS I (Fig. 5(b)). This process is called CET pathway, which is usually only about 3% of NET, but will be enhanced under stress and play a regulatory role in photosynthesis. The PET pathway is similar to the NET pathway, except that the electrons of water photohydrolysis are not transferred to NADP+, but to O2, forming superoxide anion radicals, which are eliminated by superoxide dismutase, and finally producing water (Fig. 5(b)). This process usually occurs when NADP+ is in short supply under strong light (Wang et al., 2019). As for the promotion effect of different CDs on different photosystems, it may be because the author only focused on one photosystem and did not monitor the electron transfer of two photosystems simultaneously.

Emerson et al. observed in 1957 that at far-red light (710 nm), when supplemented with red light (650 nm), quantum production increased by more than the sum of the two wavelengths of light alone. Therefore, Li, D. et al. (2020) prepared far-red CDs, which can convert UV-A into far-red light and be directly used by chloroplasts. The CDs can improve the photosynthetic rate of Rome lettuce in vivo and in vitro, thus increasing fresh weight by 51.14% and dry weight by 24.60% (Fig. 5(c)).

Carbon assimilation is the process of reducing carbon dioxide to sugars using the assimilation forces (ATP and NADPH) formed by light reactions. Organic matter, which accounts for more than 90% of plant dry weight, is converted through carbon assimilation (Fig. 5(a)). There are three biochemical pathways for carbon dioxide fixation in higher plants, including Calvin cycle (C3 pathway), C4-dicarboxylic acid pathway (C4 pathway), and the crassulaceae acid metabolism pathway (CAM pathway), among which the C3 pathway is the most basic pathway. Physiologically, C4 plants generally have stronger photosynthesis than C3 plants (Wang et al., 2019).

Tan et al. (2021) studied the effects of CDs on photosynthesis of C3 plant rice and C4 plant maize, respectively, and found that the CO2 assimilation rate of rice was 56% higher than that of control, while that of maize was only 18% higher. This was due to decreased photorespiration in C4 crops and a higher capability for CO2 assimilation. Wang, C. et al. (2021) studied the effect of N-CDs on C4 plant maize and found that foliar spraying (5 mg/L) could increase the net photosynthetic rate (21.51%) and carbohydrate content (66.43% in root and 42.03% in bud) of maize. N-CDs are an effective photoconversion material and electron donor and improve the photoelectron transfer rate.

Rubisco is one of the enzymes in the Calvin cycle that are regulated by light. Its activity can reflect the efficiency of carbon reaction to a certain extent. Studies have shown that CDs can enhance its activity and thus improve photosynthetic efficiency (Li, H et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018b; Zhang et al., 2018). The magnesium and nitrogen co-doped CDs treated rice, prepared by Li et al. (2021c) significantly improved the Rubisco activity of rice seedlings in a concentration-dependent manner, thus accelerating the assimilation of CO2. The increased activity of Rubisco can accelerate the carbon reaction process, and enhance the carbohydrate content. Zhang et al. (2018) studied the effect of chiral CDs prepared by cysteine and citric acid on mung bean and found that D-CDs increased Rubisco enzyme activity by 67.5%, while L-CDs only increased by 20.5%. Moreover, D-CDs treatment produced more carbohydrates, thus promoting plant growth.

Photosynthesis includes photoreaction and carbon reaction. It is divided into three steps, light energy absorption, electron transfer and photophosphorylation, carbon reaction. Currently, studies have been done on the role of CDs in all of these processes, but the exact role of which is dominant has not been definitively answered. Researchers prepared fluorescent N-CDs and studied the promoting mechanism of six variables related to electron transport rate (light conversion, chlorophyll content, psbA-related gene expression, adenosine triphosphate enzyme activity, NADPH production, and electron supply) on the photosystem (Wang, C. et al., 2021). Principal component analysis model was used to evaluate the contribution of each factor, and light conversion and electron supply may represent the promoting mechanism of N-CDs (Wang, C. et al., 2021). The specific theoretical mechanism needs further research.

CDs act on plant systems to improve photosynthetic efficiency mainly by optimizing the following major photosynthetic processes. (1) Optimize the absorption, transfer and conversion efficiency of light energy. This mainly includes optimizing the light-capturing antenna system, improving the transfer efficiency of electrons on the photosynthetic membrane, and enhancing the efficiency of converting light and electric energy into ATP and NADPH (Li, D et al., 2020; Li, W et al., 2018; Wang, C et al., 2021; Xiao et al., 2021). (2) Increase the efficient utilization of light energy. This mainly includes reducing energy loss such as non-photochemical quenching and enhancing photoprotection to reduce the decline of photosynthetic efficiency caused by photoinhibition (Li et al., 2021c; Swift et al., 2021). (3) Improve the efficiency of photosynthetic carbon assimilation. This mainly includes improving the carboxylation activity of Rubisco, reducing carbon loss, and reducing photorespiration (Kang and Lee, 2019; Kou et al., 2021b).

Both biotic (pathogen, insect, weed competition) and abiotic stresses (extreme temperatures, droughts, floods, high salinity, and heavy metal pollution) cause significant losses to the global production of major crops every year (Wang et al., 2003). However, the progress of cultivating stress-tolerant crop varieties has been slow, and the safety of genetically modified crops has been questioned (Zhao et al., 2020a). Therefore, a safe and sustainable new technology or approach is needed to protect plants from or alleviate adversity stress. CDs have been widely studied in the field of crop stress resistance, due to its low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and antioxidant activity ( Fig. 6).

Stress can cause a series of responses in plants, such as stress gene expression, cell metabolism and plant growth. Under the abiotic stress, the excessive accumulation of ROS in plants will lead to the destruction of cell membrane, DNA, proteins, and other cellular components, thus inhibiting plant growth (Packer and Cadenas, 2013).

CDs themselves can act as antioxidants which have the ability to remove excessive free radicals caused by abiotic stress that cause oxidative damage (Fig. 6). The surface of CDs is easy to functionalize, and the CDs rich in hydroxyl on the surface are good electron receptors. As an antioxidant, they can reduce the damage of UV irradiation on Chlorella and enhance its biomass by 18% (Zhang et al., 2018). Using salvia miltiorrhiza as a precursor, Li et al. (2021a) synthesized CDs with stronger antioxidant activity than pure salvia miltiorrhiza extracted by the hydrothermal method. The CDs showed free radical scavenging activity in plant cells and spraying the CDs on Italian lettuce seedlings under salt stress significantly reduced oxidative damage.

In addition to being an antioxidant, CDs can enhance the plant's own antioxidant defense system or induce the expression of antioxidant related enzyme genes (Li, J et al., 2019; Su et al., 2018; Xiao, L et al., 2019), thus enhancing stress resistance. Carbon citrate was prepared using the hydrothermal method by Chandrakar et al. (2020) which could reduce the absorption of heavy metal arsenic by Cicer arietinum L and reduce the toxicity of arsenic to plants by inducing the activity of enzymes and non-enzymatic antioxidants, or the expression of defense-related genes. Similar mechanisms were reported (Chen et al., 2020b,c). CA-CDs with citric acid as a precursor assisted by microwave was prepared by Li, Y. et al. (2020) and the effects of different abiotic stresses, including salt, light, and herbicides on rice cells and seedlings were systematically studied. It was found that CA-CDs enhanced the antioxidant defense system and nutrient assimilation ability of rice, thus effectively improving the stress tolerance. Recently, CDs enhanced the drought resistance of tomato seedlings by increasing the enzyme activity of antioxidant defense system, and also activated the antioxidant metabolism activity of tomato plants and up-regulated the expression of aquaporin gene was reported by Kou et al. (2021a).

In addition, Yang et al. (2021) synthesized CDs by the hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. They found that the level of root exudates and the abundance of microbial community increased to promote drought tolerance of maize. The authors suggested that root-mediated processes of rhizosphere microorganisms enhance (N and P) bioavailability and root water uptake, which contributes to maize seedling growth under drought stress.

In summary, CDs act on crops to improve their resistance to abiotic stress in three ways (Figs. 6), (1) CDs itself has antioxidant properties, which can remove excess ROS in crops caused by abiotic stress. (2) CDs can increase the activity of relevant enzymes in crop antioxidant defense system. (3) CDs can improve rhizosphere environment of crops. The first two pathways ultimately mitigate the oxidative damage caused by abiotic stress. According to the different stress types, the anti-stress mechanism initiated by crops is different, and the specific action mechanism of CDs is also different. Further research is needed to clarify this.

Biotic stress such as pathogen infection and pest attack is an important factor affecting crop yield. Although the use of pesticides can improve agricultural productivity, they pose risks to human health and the ecological environment. Rice treated with CDs showed higher resistance to sheath blight (Li et al., 2018a). CDs can enter the nucleus, loosen the structure of DNA, and induce the overexpression of the thionin gene (Os06g32600) to enhance the resistance. In addition, CDs have been widely reported to have antibacterial activity (Jun-Won and Dong-Hyun, 2021; Liu et al., 2017), which provide a possibility for their application in crop biotic stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on the post-harvest process of crops
ce:section.ce:para: In order to extend the shelf life, crops usually need to be kept fresh after harvest. Preservation methods usually include treatment by bactericide, plant growth regulator, coating agent, etc (Cheng et al., 2011). The traditional preservatives are mostly chemical synthetic drugs, which often cause residues due to improper use, causing harm to the human body and the environment. In addition, the use of commercial packaging can also play a role in preservation. The excellent optical, chemical, biological characterization and easy surface functionalization of CDs made it shine in the preservation of postharvest crops, and relevant studies were mentioned in the review (Moradi et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022).

The small size, special shape, free radical generation and scavenging properties of CDs endue its photodynamic and photothermal treatment and anti-microbial ability (Dong et al., 2020; Wang, B. et al., 2021; Xin et al., 2019). Therefore, CDs were widely used as additives or compounded with film-forming substances (PVA or SIP) for the antibacterial preservation of postharvest crops. Zhao et al. reported that soybean milk supplemented with CDs and chitosan solution effectively inhibited the growth of Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis, and significantly extended the shelf life of soybean milk at room temperature by at least 4 days (Zhao et al., 2020b). Similar results have been reported in fresh-cut cucumber (Fan et al., 2019, 2021) and mango (Xiao, D. et al., 2019) preservations. Das et al. prepared nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur co-doped CDs with antioxidant properties, and prepared PP/NPSC-dots composite films for banana storage (Das et al., 2021). It was found that on the fifth day, compared with the blank and PP films, the bananas preserved by the composite film were still intact and decomposed without defects.

During the storage process of crops, exposure to UV will accelerate their spoilage and deterioration. The use of UV-blocking substances in packaging materials can slow down this process. CDs have the characteristic of absorbing UV light, therefore, UV-resistant packaging materials based on CDs have been widely studied. A highly transparent, flexible polyvinyl alcohol/tea residue CDs composite film was prepared for UV blocking (Patil et al., 2020). The UV barrier ability of the composite film increased with the increase of CDs concentration in the film. The barrier rate of UV-C and UV-B was 100%. It was used to keep grape fruits fresh. After 30 h UV irradiation, the grapes in the control group were brown and shriveled, while the grapes in the composite film treatment group were intact without significant changes. Packaging materials with similar effects include: Bacterial nanocellulose paper soaked in CDs solution (Salimi et al., 2021), polyvinylidene fluoride film soaked in CQDs/PVA solution pretreated with alkaline solution (Dong et al., 2019), and PVA film prepared by chitosan and CDs (prepared with acorn-cup as precursor) (Xu et al., 2021).

Based on the excellent fluorescence quenching and recovery properties of CDs, their applications in pesticide and veterinary drug residue detection in agricultural products have been widely reported and reviewed (Sun et al., 2020). For example, the CDs encapsulated molecularly imprinted fluorescent quenching particles prepared by Shao et al. can detect zearalenone in corn samples with a detection range of 0.02–1.0 mg/L and a detection limit of 0.02 mg/L (Shao et al., 2018). Recent studies have found that immobilizing the prepared CDs on the PVA carrier can stabilize the fluorescence of CDs and inhibit the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of CDs over time (Hu et al., 2022). In the range of 0–350 μM, the quenched fluorescence intensity of CDs showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of tetracycline, thus enabling the detection of tetracycline in complex agricultural samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comprehensive and high-value utilization of crop waste
ce:section.ce:para: At the end of the crops life cycle, the waste left behind, such as straw and rotten leaf peel, is usually used for landfill, incineration, or as feed and fertilizer, with low utilization rate (Duan et al., 2021). Crop waste is rich in phytochemical constituents, which can be used as biomass materials to form high-value products (including nanomaterials, drugs, chemicals, fuels and biological materials, etc.) (Monaliben et al., 2015). Rice husks, bagasse and wheat straw are used as green and readily available precursors for the production of fluorescent silicon nanomaterials (Bose et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2016). The precursors, synthesis conditions, properties and applications of CDs prepared from agricultural wastes (including plant leaf extracts, discarded fruit and vegetable peels, banana pseudo stem, and litchi pulp, etc.) were listed in Table 2 (Omran et al., 2021). Apart from that, for example, by using the waste meal produced by oilseed press-cake with protein removal as the precursor, the fluorescent film with antioxidant properties was prepared by the hydrothermal carbonization process, which can be used as forgery-proof packaging (Purkayastha et al., 2014). Using rice husk as a precursor, Thongsai et al. (2019) prepared CDs with good photostability and blue emission by hydrothermal and calcination methods. The CDs can detect alcohol vapor at room temperature and can distinguish several volatile organic compounds when used as a sensing layer in an optical electronic nose system ( Fig. 7(a)). Similarly, with bitter tea residue (Hu et al., 2021) and coconut petiole residues (Gao et al., 2021) as precursors, the prepared CDs can be used for the detection of metal ions. It is worth noting that there is still a huge space for the improvement of yield and quantum yield by using agricultural waste as the precursor to prepare CDs. Using corn cobs as the precursor, CDs with a yield of up to 55% was prepared by Wu et al. (2021). Based on the fluorescence quenching principle, CDs were used for Fe3+ selective detection, which had a good linear correlation within the concentration range of 0–120 mu M (Fig. 7(b)). Li, Z. et al. (2021) used corn stalk shell as a precursor to prepare CDs with good fluorescence performance, stability and biocompatibility by the near-critical water pyrolysis method, which could be used for detection and biological imaging (Fig. 7(c)).

CDs show great potential in addressing climate change, population growth, and improving crop yields. This review details the role of CDs on the entire crop life cycle and the cycle of CDs re-entering the crop life cycle as precursors from crop waste. The advantages, disadvantages and challenges of CDs application to the crop life cycle were summarized in Table 3. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs and the expression of the gene regulating water pore protein make CDs have the characteristics of promoting seed germination. The carbon content of CDs is over 60%, and it can combine with nitrogenase and enhance its activity, thus promoting the nitrogen fixation of plants and indirectly acting as nitrogen fertilizer. Their small size and positive surface charge give them the ability to deliver DNA or RNA into plant cells. The luminous wavelength range of CDs can be adjusted by changing the precursor or reaction conditions to cover the absorption region of plant chlorophyll, so as to act as a light-catching antenna to promote plant growth. CDs with antioxidant properties can reduce oxidative damage caused by adversity stress in plants, or improve the antioxidant defense system and nutrient assimilation ability of plants, thus improving the stress resistance of plants. In addition, some CDs can improve plant stress resistance by changing the composition and abundance of rhizosphere microorganisms. There is no clear and unanimous conclusion about which working mode (hydroponics, soil cultivation and foliar spraying) is the most effective. More attention should be paid to the effect of CDs on root microorganisms in soil culture. The potential mechanism of CDs enhancing plant stress resistance still needs further studied by omics or other methods.

CDs are used in the packaging of postharvest agricultural products and can modulate the mechanical, barrier and optical properties of the film to protect agricultural products through antioxidant or antibacterial activity. Unique labels can also be formed to prevent adulteration, or to reflect changes in quality during storage. Although most reports suggest a certain concentration of CDs had no toxicity on cell models or animal models in the short term (Guo et al., 2022). In addition, studies have reported the CDs were discovered in the processing of food-derived substances such as roasted meat, coffee, beer (Wang, H. et al., 2020). However, the migration of CDs to the human gastrointestinal tract through packaging and the impact on the sensory properties of agricultural products need to be considered. Acute and long-term toxicity to humans and the environment through bioaccumulation and biotransformation needs to be fully identified before CDs can act. Risk assessment currently focuses on the preparation and field application of CDs. A more comprehensive life cycle analysis, which also takes into account the production, transportation, disposal and associated energy and water use of CDs, would also provide useful information. And in addition to studying the toxicity of CDs in its original form, the effects of accumulation, change and transformation of CDs on the environment and human body should also be considered. Natural crop waste is rich in C, N, O, S, and is used to synthesize CDs, which is green and environmentally friendly, without doping. The main limitation is the complexity of the source material composition, which requires purification dialysis before and after processing to ensure the purity of the CDs. Additionally, the production yield and quantum efficiency of crop waste derived CDs need to be improved.
